questions.specification:
A cross-sectional study involving over 200 elite and sub-elite endurance athletes examined their preference for various recovery modalities. The aim was to understand patterns in usage and rationale for techniques such as massage, sleep, compression garments, and cold water immersion, based on sex, event type, and competition level.

Figures 1 to 3 summarize the reported use of these methods across different population segments.

Figure 1: Recovery Modalities: Sex-Based Usage  

  
![](/api/r2/quiz-image?key=zfD39eB0fP3jWel-ifkZY)

Figure 2: Recovery Modalities by Discipline

![](/api/r2/quiz-image?key=fy5N4uLtcd1N18NxjSf-I)

Figure 3: Recovery Modalities by Competitive Tier

![](/api/r2/quiz-image?key=e4Uv62csjTB6NEmHbqZZQ)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is centered on athlete recovery modalities (massage, sleep/nap, compression garments, cold water immersion, sauna), comparing usage patterns across sex/discipline/level and evaluating recovery strategies—directly aligned with recovery techniques.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.2 Maintaining homeostasis > A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts asking for physiological benefits of sauna and trends in cold water immersion relate to body responses to thermal stress and returning toward equilibrium, which links to homeostasis mechanisms.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.4 Stress and coping > C.4.2 Coping > C.4.2.1 Coping strategies: problem-focused, emotion-focused, and avoidance-focused approaches
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.2.1 Coping strategies: problem-focused, emotion-focused, and avoidance-focused approaches
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The final synthesis includes evaluating sleep-related strategies as ‘essential’ and includes a psychological dimension of recovery; this can overlap with coping/recovery behaviours, though it is secondary to physical recovery strategies.

parts.content: Based on Figure 1, which recovery method was most widely adopted overall, and by how many more men than women used this method?
parts.markscheme:
- Most used method overall: Sauna bathing <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Used by ~90 men and ~55 women → difference = approximately 35 athletes <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Accept 34–36 range

<Note> If a candidate clearly justifies an alternative “most used” method due to very similar bar heights (within ~5 athletes), award the first mark.

<Note> Allow reasonable rounding from the y-axis scale when calculating the difference.

parts.content: Using Figure 1 only, identify the recovery method with the lowest reported usage and suggest one reason it may be less preferred.
parts.markscheme:
- Least used method (Figure 1): Compression garments OR Cold water immersion (accept the lowest as read from the figure) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Possible reason (any one): cost; limited access/facilities; discomfort; time required/logistical difficulty; lack of perceived effectiveness/evidence; interference with training/routines <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Accept other plausible reasons linked to uptake.

parts.content: Of racewalkers, middle-distance, and long-distance runners, which reported the highest uptake of sauna bathing?
parts.markscheme:
- Group with highest sauna use: Racewalking athletes <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> If two groups are essentially equal based on the plotted values, accept either with clear reference to the figure.

parts.content: Outline one physiological benefit and one psychological benefit associated with sauna use in recovery.
parts.markscheme:
- Physiological (any one): increased blood flow/vasodilation; reduced muscle soreness via relaxation of muscle; improved thermoregulatory adaptations; possible increased plasma volume; improved range of motion/soft tissue extensibility <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Psychological (any one): relaxation; reduced stress/anxiety; improved perceived recovery/well-being; improved sleep quality; reduced perceived fatigue <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Accept other valid benefits.

parts.content: Describe the trend in the use of cold water immersion from national to elite-level athletes.
parts.markscheme:
- Cold water immersion use declines with competition level (national → international → elite) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Highest in national-level athletes, lowest in elite athletes <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Accept descriptions such as “moderate decrease”/“gradual decline” if consistent with the figure, or approximate values quoted from the bars.

parts.content: Synthesize data across all three figures. To what extent does the data support the claim that sleep-related strategies (daytime nap and long night sleep) are essential components of athlete recovery?
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies that sleep-related methods (daytime naps and/or long night sleep) show high reported use across the segments presented <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Uses data from more than one figure to support the claim (e.g., consistent high uptake across sex, discipline, and/or competition tier) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Links sleep to recovery outcomes (e.g., physical restoration such as tissue repair/glycogen restoration and/or psychological restoration such as mood/cognitive recovery) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Credit evaluative balance (to support “to what extent”): acknowledges that other modalities also have high uptake and/or that self-reported, cross-sectional data cannot establish necessity/causation.

questions.specification:
Using the image, what is the primary physiological reason that sleeping in alignment with the circadian rhythm enhances recovery?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ox29-u8yeYHa4sw7ONO1k)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks why aligning sleep with circadian rhythm enhances recovery, emphasizing physiological recovery mechanisms (e.g., growth hormone release, tissue repair), which fits exercise recovery strategies/techniques.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.1 Inter-system communication > A.1.1.2 The endocrine system and regulation of biological processes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1.2 The endocrine system and regulation of biological processes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Circadian rhythm effects on recovery are largely mediated by endocrine regulation (e.g., nighttime growth hormone secretion), directly linking to endocrine system regulation of biological processes.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.2 Maintaining homeostasis > A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Circadian alignment relates to maintaining homeostasis via biological clocks and day–night regulation, which supports recovery through stable internal conditions.

options.content: Sleep aligned with circadian rhythm lowers blood pressure and improves creatine synthesis.
options.markscheme: While sleep can influence blood pressure, creatine synthesis occurs primarily in the liver and kidneys and is not the primary mechanism of circadian-enhanced recovery.

options.content: Circadian alignment promotes polyphenol absorption and muscle glycogen synthesis.
options.markscheme: Polyphenol absorption and muscle glycogen synthesis are primarily dictated by dietary intake and post-exercise recovery protocols rather than the circadian timing of sleep.

options.content: Nighttime circadian alignment boosts growth hormone release and tissue repair.
options.markscheme: The largest pulses of growth hormone (GH) are secreted during slow-wave sleep (SWS). Aligning sleep with the natural circadian cycle (nighttime) maximizes SWS and the associated GH release, which supports protein synthesis and the repair of muscle and other tissues.

options.content: Early-morning circadian peaks increase $\text{VO}_2\text{ max}$ and electrolyte retention.
options.markscheme: $\text{VO}_2\text{ max}$ is a measure of aerobic capacity and does not peak in the early morning to facilitate recovery; additionally, electrolyte retention is regulated by hormonal signals like aldosterone, not primarily by sleep-circadian alignment.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1.1 Importance of water and electrolyte balance for body functioning
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question focuses on fatigue risk in hot environments driven by heat stress and fluid loss through sweating—core concepts of water and electrolyte balance and dehydration effects on performance.

options.content: Hyperhydration
Heat stress and fluid loss through sweating
options.markscheme: B

options.content: Reduced blood volume (plasma volume) due to dehydration
Decreased electrolyte absorption

questions.specification: Which physiological process is a characteristic of the fast (alactacid) component of excess post-exercise oxygen consumption (EPOC)?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.4 Excess post-exercise oxygen consumption (EPOC) and recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.4 Excess post-exercise oxygen consumption (EPOC) and recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks about the fast (alactacid) component of EPOC and which recovery process characterizes it (e.g., phosphocreatine resynthesis), which is covered under EPOC and recovery.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Understanding the fast/alactacid EPOC component relies on knowledge of the phosphagen (ATP-PC) energy system and phosphocreatine restoration, which is part of energy production through phosphagen/glycolytic/oxidative systems.

options.content: Resynthesis of muscle phosphocreatine stores
options.markscheme: Correct. Fast (alactacid) EPOC is primarily used to restore ATP-PC stores (including phosphocreatine resynthesis) and re-oxygenate myoglobin/haemoglobin.

options.content: Metabolic oxidation of accumulated lactate
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Lactate removal/oxidation is associated with the slow (lactacid) component of EPOC, not the fast component.

options.content: Restoration of the body's resting core temperature
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Elevated body temperature contributes to the slow component of EPOC due to increased metabolic rate and thermoregulatory demands.

options.content: Full replenishment of liver and muscle glycogen
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Glycogen resynthesis is a longer-term recovery process and aligns with the slow component rather than the fast (alactacid) component.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for primary causes of fatigue in a 100m sprint, which targets physiological sources/mechanisms of fatigue (e.g., phosphocreatine depletion, metabolite accumulation, neuromuscular factors).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Explaining sprint fatigue typically requires linking to dominant energy systems in a 100m (phosphagen and fast glycolysis) and how their limits contribute to fatigue.

parts.content: Discuss primary causes of fatigue experienced by a 100m sprinter.
parts.markscheme:
Possible causes of fatigue for a 100m sprinter:

- Depletion of muscle glycogen/energy stores <marks>1 mark</marks>
- During intense exercise, muscle glycogen is rapidly used up
Accumulation of lactic acid/hydrogen ions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Depletion of phosphocreatine stores <marks>1 mark</marks>
- diet / dehydration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- perception of fatigue will differ between individuals <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
With reference to the supplement label shown, which claim is most scientifically supported about creatine monohydrate’s role in post-exercise recovery?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/X3YRSBlKpOOCFozyvPyaG)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The options focus on phosphocreatine/ATP resynthesis and distinguishing energy-system claims (phosphagen vs oxidative), which aligns with energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic and oxidative systems.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about a supplement’s role in exercise recovery (e.g., DOMS, post-exercise processes), which fits strategies/techniques for exercise recovery.

options.content: Enhances slow-twitch muscle fiber recruitment during long-duration endurance events
options.markscheme: Creatine supplementation is primarily associated with increased phosphocreatine availability and improved performance in short-duration, high-intensity exercise rather than directly enhancing slow-twitch recruitment in endurance events.

options.content: Increases phosphocreatine stores to support rapid ATP resynthesis in high-intensity efforts
options.markscheme: Creatine monohydrate increases intramuscular creatine/phosphocreatine stores, which supports faster ATP resynthesis via the phosphagen system, aiding repeated high-intensity efforts and potentially improving recovery between bouts.

options.content: Directly decreases delayed-onset muscle soreness (DOMS) after aerobic training
options.markscheme: Evidence for creatine directly reducing DOMS is inconsistent; any effect is not as well supported as its role in increasing phosphocreatine availability and ATP resynthesis capacity.

options.content: Reduces cortisol levels post-exercise, promoting faster glycogen resynthesis
options.markscheme: Creatine’s primary supported mechanism is increased phosphocreatine availability; it is not reliably supported as a supplement that reduces cortisol to accelerate glycogen resynthesis.

parts.content: Outline the various types of fatigue athletes may experience.
parts.markscheme:
- peripheral fatigue: Inability of muscles to continue contracting effectively due to depletion of energy sources or accumulation of metabolic waste products. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Central/mental fatigue: Decreased alertness, focus, and motivation due to prolonged physical or mental exertion impairing the central nervous system <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.markscheme:

- Recovery is needed to achieve physiological adaptations <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept any other valid physiological reasons for recovery</note>

questions.specification: Which metabolic/biochemical change within the skeletal muscle fibre (intracellular) is a major contributor to peripheral fatigue during high-intensity exercise?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question targets peripheral fatigue mechanisms during high-intensity exercise, specifically metabolic factors like increased H+ concentration contributing to fatigue.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Increased H+ accumulation is linked to high-intensity glycolytic energy production, so understanding the glycolytic energy system supports answering the question.

options.content: Reduction in the firing rate of the motor neurons
options.markscheme: Incorrect — this reflects central/neural factors rather than an intracellular metabolic/biochemical change in the muscle fibre.

options.content: Increased concentration of hydrogen ions ($H^+$)
options.markscheme: Correct (B).

options.content: Depletion of intramuscular glycogen stores
options.markscheme: Incorrect — glycogen depletion contributes more to fatigue in longer-duration exercise than acute high-intensity bouts.

options.content: Decreased recruitment of motor units by the brain
options.markscheme: Incorrect — this is a central (CNS) contribution, not a metabolic/biochemical change within the muscle fibre.

Phosphocreatine depletion and incomplete recovery between sprints
Accumulation of $H^+$ (reduced pH) impairing glycolytic enzyme activity and muscle contraction

questions.specification:
Refer to the graph showing body temperature fluctuations across a $24\text{-hour}$ period.

An elite swimmer performs a time trial at $07:30$ and again at $18:00$. The athlete reports feeling more sluggish during the morning trial despite equal sleep hours the previous night.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/Cq-fKAuDtLwBxRqsY7zMI)

Which physiological factor most likely contributed to the lower morning performance?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.2 Maintaining homeostasis > A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question uses a 24-hour body-temperature fluctuation graph and asks why morning performance is lower; this is circadian/diurnal variation in core temperature as part of homeostatic regulation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.1 Inter-system communication > A.1.1.2 The endocrine system and regulation of biological processes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1.2 The endocrine system and regulation of biological processes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Options reference melatonin and circadian hormonal influences, implicating endocrine regulation of biological processes contributing to time-of-day performance differences.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.1 Inter-system communication > A.1.1.1 The nervous system and physiological response coordination
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1.1 The nervous system and physiological response coordination
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The correct option mentions neuromuscular coordination, linking time-of-day physiological state (lower core temperature) to nervous-system control of movement/performance.

options.content: Core body temperature is relatively low in the morning, affecting neuromuscular coordination.
options.markscheme: Body temperature follows a circadian rhythm, reaching a minimum in the early morning (typically around $04:00$) and peaking in the early evening. At $07:30$, core body temperature is still significantly lower than at $18:00$. This lower temperature reduces enzyme activity and nerve conduction velocity, which impairs neuromuscular coordination and overall physical performance.

options.content: Blood lactate levels are higher in the morning.
options.markscheme: Resting blood lactate levels do not typically show a morning peak that would explain sluggishness; in fact, they remain relatively stable or are slightly lower at rest in the morning compared to the evening.

options.content: Increased melatonin secretion stimulates early physical performance.
options.markscheme: Melatonin is a hormone that induces sleep and its levels are higher at night. Increased melatonin secretion would inhibit rather than stimulate physical performance.

options.content: Circadian rhythm promotes muscle peak force output before sunrise.
options.markscheme: Muscle peak force output generally follows the core body temperature curve, which peaks in the late afternoon or early evening, not before sunrise.

questions.specification: Which of the following best describes peripheral fatigue originating within the working skeletal muscle (not the CNS) during high-intensity cycling?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to identify peripheral fatigue in high-intensity cycling, which is characterized by local muscular mechanisms such as metabolite (lactate/H+) accumulation—covered under sources and mechanisms of fatigue.

options.content: Failure of motor neurons to generate action potentials
Lack of oxygen transport to the brain
Accumulation of metabolites like lactate and hydrogen ions ($\text{H}^+$) in muscles
Decreased psychological motivation

questions.specification: Which metabolite accumulation is a key cause of peripheral fatigue during high-intensity anaerobic exercise?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about a physiological cause of peripheral fatigue during high-intensity anaerobic activity (e.g., H+ accumulation), which is directly within mechanisms/sources of fatigue.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Peripheral fatigue in high-intensity anaerobic work is closely tied to glycolytic energy system byproducts (H+), so knowledge of anaerobic energy production supports answering.

options.content: An increase in the concentration of hydrogen ions ($H^+$)
options.markscheme: Correct: A — increased $H^+$ (reduced pH) is associated with peripheral fatigue during high-intensity anaerobic exercise.

options.content: A decrease in the levels of inorganic phosphate ($P_i$)
options.markscheme: Incorrect: $P_i$ tends to accumulate (increase), not decrease, during high-intensity exercise and contributes to peripheral fatigue when elevated.

options.content: The depletion of liver glycogen stores
options.markscheme: Incorrect: liver glycogen depletion is more associated with prolonged exercise and central/blood glucose effects rather than acute peripheral fatigue in high-intensity anaerobic bouts.

options.content: A reduction in the neural drive from the motor cortex
options.markscheme: Incorrect: reduced neural drive is primarily a central fatigue mechanism, not peripheral fatigue.

questions.specification: Why is dehydration a significant contributor to fatigue in hot environments?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.1 Water and electrolyte balance > A.2.1.1 Importance of water and electrolyte balance for body functioning
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1.1 Importance of water and electrolyte balance for body functioning
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question focuses on dehydration in hot environments and its effects (reduced plasma volume, impaired thermoregulation), which is central to water and electrolyte balance in body functioning.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It asks why dehydration contributes to fatigue, linking hydration status to mechanisms/sources of fatigue (particularly in heat stress contexts).

options.content: It reduces stroke volume and increases heart rate (cardiovascular drift).
options.markscheme: Dehydration does not primarily *increase* performance capacity; this option describes a consequence that can contribute to fatigue but is not the best answer compared with reduced plasma volume and impaired thermoregulation.

options.content: It reduces plasma volume and thermoregulation efficiency.
options.markscheme: Correct: dehydration lowers plasma volume, reducing skin blood flow and sweating, impairing heat dissipation and increasing cardiovascular strain, contributing to fatigue.

options.content: It increases the risk of hypothermia by increasing evaporative heat loss.
options.markscheme: In hot environments, dehydration typically reduces sweat rate/evaporative cooling and increases the risk of hyperthermia, not hypothermia.

options.content: It increases oxygen diffusion in the lungs, leading to earlier fatigue.
options.markscheme: Dehydration in heat is not primarily linked to pulmonary oxygen diffusion limitations; fatigue is more strongly related to reduced plasma volume, cardiovascular strain, and impaired thermoregulation.

options.content: Low-intensity swimming (active recovery)
options.markscheme: Correct: active recovery maintains blood flow and oxygen delivery, accelerating lactate oxidation/clearance compared with passive or other recovery modalities.

options.content: Sitting poolside (passive recovery)
options.markscheme: Incorrect: passive recovery reduces muscle pump and blood flow, generally slowing lactate clearance versus active recovery.

options.content: Immediate consumption of high-glycemic carbohydrates
options.markscheme: Incorrect: carbohydrate intake may support glycogen resynthesis but does not produce the fastest lactate clearance within the first 15 minutes compared with active recovery.

options.content: Total body immersion in an ice bath (cryotherapy)
options.markscheme: Incorrect: cold immersion can reduce blood flow and may impair lactate clearance relative to active recovery in the early recovery period.

questions.specification: Which mechanism explains delayed-onset muscle soreness (DOMS)?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: DOMS is a post-exercise phenomenon linked to muscle damage and inflammatory response, fitting mechanisms of fatigue/recovery (microtears rather than lactate).

options.content: Lactic acid accumulation
options.markscheme: Incorrect: lactate is cleared relatively quickly after exercise and does not explain DOMS (which peaks 24–72 hours later).

options.content: Microtears in muscle fibres causing inflammation
options.markscheme: Correct: eccentric exercise can cause microtrauma to muscle/connective tissue leading to an inflammatory response and soreness in the following days.

options.content: Muscle spasm after exercise
options.markscheme: Incorrect: muscle spasm has been suggested historically but is not the accepted primary mechanism for DOMS.

options.content: Accumulation of hydrogen ions (H+) and other metabolic by-products
options.markscheme: Incorrect: acute metabolite accumulation contributes to immediate fatigue/burning sensation, not delayed soreness characteristic of DOMS.

parts.content: Describe two common cryotherapy methods used for recovery.
parts.markscheme:
### Method 1: Whole body cooling (WBC) / Cold water immersion (CWI)
- immersing part/all of the body in cold water ($10\text{--}15\,^{\circ}\text{C}$) for a period of time ($10\text{--}20\text{ minutes}$) <marks>1</marks>

### Method 2: Applying ice packs/cold packs
- placing ice/gel packs on the affected area for a period of time ($15\text{--}20\text{ minutes}$) <marks>1</marks>

### Method 3: Contrast water therapy (CWT)
- exposing the body or body parts to hot and cold water treatments <marks>1</marks>

<note>Award [1] max for each method described up to [2]. Accept any other appropriate cryotherapy methods.</note>

questions.specification:
Which recovery strategy is most likely to produce faster blood lactate clearance following exercise?

| Time post-exercise (min) | Blood lactate concentration (mmol L⁻¹) Group A (active recovery) | Blood lactate concentration (mmol L⁻¹) Group B (passive recovery) |
|-------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 0                       | 10.5                                                             | 10.5                                                              |
| 15                      | 6.4                                                              | 8.1                                                               |
| 30                      | 3.2                                                              | 6.9                                                               |
| 45                      | 1.8                                                              | 5.4                                                               |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question compares active vs passive recovery using post-exercise lactate values and asks for the physiological explanation (active recovery increases circulation and lactate clearance), which is directly about recovery strategies/techniques.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.4 Excess post-exercise oxygen consumption (EPOC) and recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.4 Excess post-exercise oxygen consumption (EPOC) and recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Interpreting post-exercise recovery data and mechanisms overlaps with EPOC/recovery physiology, though the prompt is more specifically about lactate clearance than oxygen debt.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Lactate production/clearance is tied to glycolytic vs oxidative energy system contributions; however, the focus is recovery method rather than energy system identification.

options.content: Group A; active recovery enhances lactate clearance via increased circulation.
options.markscheme: Correct: Group A shows lower blood lactate at 15, 30 and 45 min, consistent with faster lactate clearance during active recovery due to maintained blood flow.

options.content: Group A; passive recovery enhances mitochondrial efficiency.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: passive recovery is not the recovery strategy used by Group A, and mitochondrial efficiency is not the primary acute explanation for faster lactate clearance shown in the data.

options.content: Group B; increased blood flow during passive recovery speeds lactate clearance.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: the data show Group B has higher blood lactate at each time point after 0 min, indicating slower (not faster) lactate clearance; passive recovery does not increase blood flow compared with active recovery.

options.content: Group B; reduced heart rate accelerates lactate buffering.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: Group B demonstrates slower lactate clearance in the table, and “lactate buffering”/heart rate reduction does not explain a faster reduction in blood lactate here.

questions.specification:
The table shows the primary limiting factor associated with fatigue for two different athletic events:

| Athlete | Event | Duration | Primary limiting factor | 
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | 
| 1 | Marathon | 2–4 hours | Depletion of muscle glycogen | 
| 2 | 800 m run | 1.5–2 minutes | **[X]** | 

Which physiological change correctly replaces **[X]** in the table?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.1 Sources and mechanisms of fatigue (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the primary limiting factor/mechanism of fatigue for an 800 m run (1.5–2 min), which centers on physiological fatigue mechanisms such as H+ accumulation from high-rate anaerobic glycolysis.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Identifying fatigue in an 800 m event requires understanding which energy system predominates (glycolytic with some phosphagen/oxidative contribution) and associated byproducts (e.g., H+), linking to energy production systems.

options.content: Accumulation of $H^+$ ions
options.markscheme: High-intensity exercise lasting 1 to 2 minutes relies heavily on anaerobic glycolysis, which results in the accumulation of lactic acid and hydrogen ions ($H^+$). This leads to a decrease in muscle pH (acidosis), which inhibits enzymatic activity and interferes with muscle contraction.

options.content: Restoration of muscle phosphocreatine (PCr)
options.markscheme: The restoration of phosphocreatine is a recovery process that occurs during the fast component of excess post-exercise oxygen consumption (EPOC), not a cause of fatigue during the event.

options.content: Increased levels of serotonin in the brain
options.markscheme: Changes in neurotransmitter levels, such as increased serotonin, are associated with central fatigue, which typically develops during prolonged, sub-maximal endurance activities rather than short-duration high-intensity events.

options.content: Depletion of muscle phosphocreatine (PCr)
options.markscheme: PCr is a primary anaerobic energy store that can become substantially reduced during very high-intensity efforts; however, for an 800 m run (1.5–2 minutes) fatigue is more commonly attributed to the high reliance on anaerobic glycolysis and the associated accumulation of $H^+$ (metabolic acidosis) rather than PCr depletion alone.

questions.specification: Why does glycogen depletion limit performance in endurance exercise?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks why glycogen depletion limits endurance performance, focusing on substrate use and ATP production rate (shift toward slower fat oxidation), which is covered under energy production via oxidative/glycolytic systems.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.2 Fuelling for health and performance > A.2.2.1 Macronutrients and their role in energy maintenance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2.1 Macronutrients and their role in energy maintenance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It also relates to carbohydrate (glycogen) as a macronutrient fuel source and consequences of low glycogen availability during endurance exercise.

options.content: It forces reliance on fat, which produces ATP more slowly.
options.markscheme: Correct: glycogen depletion increases reliance on fat oxidation, which has a slower rate of ATP resynthesis at exercise intensities typical of endurance performance.

options.content: It decreases the rate of ATP resynthesis because fat oxidation requires more oxygen per ATP produced.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: fat oxidation does require more oxygen per ATP than carbohydrate, but this is not the primary reason performance is limited; the key limitation is the slower rate of ATP resynthesis when relying more on fat.

options.content: It increases lactic acid clearance.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: glycogen depletion does not increase lactate clearance; if anything, lower carbohydrate availability reduces glycolytic flux and lactate production rather than improving clearance.

options.content: It reduces calcium uptake in muscles.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: while fatigue can involve impaired calcium handling, glycogen depletion is primarily linked to reduced carbohydrate availability and a slower ATP production rate rather than directly reducing calcium uptake.

questions.specification:
The table shows an athlete's readiness score tracked over $5$ nights using wearable technology. It includes hours of sleep, heart rate variability ($\text{HRV}$), and a muscle soreness subjective rating scale ($0\text{--}10$).

| Night | Sleep duration ($h$) | $\text{HRV}$ (ms) | Soreness score (subjective rating, $0\text{--}10$) | Readiness score (\%) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $1$ | $6.2$ | $38$ | $7$ | $42$ |
| $2$ | $7.1$ | $43$ | $6$ | $53$ |
| $3$ | $7.9$ | $50$ | $4$ | $68$ |
| $4$ | $8.2$ | $57$ | $2$ | $84$ |
| $5$ | $8.4$ | $61$ | $1$ | $91$ |

Which option best describes the trend in the data and its implication for recovery?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.3 Response > A.3.3 Fatigue and recovery (HL) > A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question interprets trends in sleep duration, HRV, soreness, and readiness to explain improved post-exercise recovery—directly assessing recovery strategies/indicators (e.g., sleep and autonomic recovery via HRV).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.1 Communication > A.1.2 Maintaining homeostasis > A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: HRV reflects autonomic nervous system balance and recovery/homeostatic regulation; the question links parasympathetic recovery to improved readiness.

options.content: Decreased soreness with increased $\text{HRV}\rightarrow$ indicates physical and parasympathetic recovery
options.markscheme: Correct. As sleep duration increases ($6.2$ to $8.4\,h$), $\text{HRV}$ increases ($38$ to $61\,\text{ms}$) and soreness decreases ($7$ to $1$), consistent with improved recovery (higher $\text{HRV}$ and lower soreness).

options.content: Increased sleep duration raises soreness $\rightarrow$ delayed onset muscle fatigue
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The data shows soreness decreases as sleep duration increases (from $7$ down to $1$).

options.content: Readiness score correlates with sleep only $\rightarrow$ $\text{HRV}$ is not linked to recovery
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Readiness score increases alongside both sleep duration and $\text{HRV}$ (e.g., $42\%$ to $91\%$ while $\text{HRV}$ rises $38$ to $61\,\text{ms}$).

options.content: Higher $\text{HRV}$ suppresses recovery $\rightarrow$ indicates sympathetic dominance
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Higher $\text{HRV}$ is generally associated with increased parasympathetic activity and improved recovery state, not sympathetic dominance.

questions.specification: Following an intensive endurance event, an athlete must focus on the rapid resynthesis of muscle glycogen. Which of the following macronutrients is the essential substrate for this recovery process?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.2 Fuelling for health and performance > A.2.2.1 Macronutrients and their role in energy maintenance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2.1 Macronutrients and their role in energy maintenance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which macronutrient is the essential substrate for rapid muscle glycogen resynthesis after endurance exercise—this is directly about carbohydrates as a macronutrient and its role in recovery/energy maintenance.

options.content: Lipids
Carbohydrates
Proteins
Water

questions.specification: Why is active recovery often recommended after intense exercise?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.3.2 Strategies and techniques for exercise recovery (HL)

options.content: It reduces muscle temperature quickly.
It enhances lactate removal and circulation.
It decreases glycogen resynthesis.
It reduces delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS) by stopping lactate production.
